data.title: Bitcoin ATMs Emerge in Nairobi Amid Regulatory Challenges
data.description:
According to Cointelegraph, Bitcoin ATMs have been installed in major shopping malls across Nairobi shortly after Kenya enacted its first comprehensive cryptocurrency law. This development presents an immediate challenge for regulators, as no crypto provider has yet been authorized to operate under the new legislation. Local media outlet Capital News reported that these machines, branded 'Bankless Bitcoin,' have been placed alongside traditional banking kiosks, offering cash-to-crypto services to the public.

The introduction of Bitcoin ATMs is not entirely new to Kenya. In 2018, The East African reported that BitClub installed Bitcoin ATMs in Nairobi, although their adoption was limited, and they did not penetrate mainstream retail spaces. CoinATMradar data shows that there are currently only two reported Bitcoin ATMs in the country. The recent installations coincide with the implementation of Kenya’s Virtual Assets Service Providers Act of 2025, which came into effect on November 4. This act established a formal licensing framework for wallet operators, exchanges, custodians, and other crypto platforms.

Under the new law, the Central Bank of Kenya (CBK) is tasked with overseeing payment and custody functions, while the Capital Markets Authority (CMA) regulates investment and trading activities. Despite the law being in place, the necessary regulations to initiate the licensing of Virtual Asset Service Providers (VASPs) have not yet been issued. Consequently, providers are currently operating without the required licenses. In a joint notice, the CBK and CMA emphasized that no VASP has been licensed under the new laws to operate in or from Kenya, warning that any claims of authorization are illegal.

The presence of Bitcoin ATMs in upscale malls highlights the expansion of Kenya’s informal crypto ecosystem, even as it operates in a regulatory gray area. Capital News noted that while these ATMs are just beginning to appear in more affluent areas, Bitcoin usage has been prevalent in lower-income neighborhoods like Kibera. In these areas, residents use Bitcoin as an alternative form of banking. AfriBit Africa co-founder Ronnie Mdawida explained that Bitcoin allows residents to hold value without the need for documentation or banking paperwork, offering what he described as 'financial freedom' for those living on minimal income.

data.title: Analysts: Stablecoins are unlikely to grow to a level sufficient to absorb US government debt.
data.description: Berenberg economist Atakan Bakiskan stated that stablecoins are unlikely to grow to a level sufficient to absorb significant amounts of U.S. government debt or compete with bank deposits. Because the Genius Act prohibits issuers from paying interest, stablecoins cannot compete with interest-bearing bank accounts, thus reducing their attractiveness. Furthermore, if funds flow from deposits to Treasury-backed stablecoins, banks' ability to lend or purchase government bonds could decline.

data.title: K33: Factors such as ETF outflows and long-term holders selling exacerbated Bitcoin selling pressure.
data.description: In a new report, Vetle Lunde, Head of Research at K33, stated that open interest among perpetual futures traders increased by over 36,000 bitcoins, the largest weekly increase since April 2023. Simultaneously, funding rates are also climbing, suggesting that traders are engaging in "grabbing the knife" behavior rather than defensive positioning. The rising funding rates may stem from the execution of limit orders that were intended to trigger a rapid price rebound and break below the six-month low. However, this rebound failed to materialize, and this leverage effect now means excess funds, increasing the risk of heightened market volatility due to liquidations. Furthermore, Bitcoin ETFs have also experienced a sell-off, with 20,150 bitcoins flowing out of related products in the past week and nearly 40,000 bitcoins in the past 30 days. Six of the past seven ETF trading days ended with outflows, including a single-day outflow of 10,060 bitcoins on November 13th, factors that exacerbated the selling pressure on Bitcoin. (The Block)

data.title: Data leaked by the U.S. Department of Labor due to a "technical glitch": Number of people continuing to claim unemployment benefits surges.
data.description: Data released by the U.S. government on Tuesday showed a sharp increase in the number of Americans claiming unemployment benefits between mid-September and mid-October, suggesting that the unemployment rate may have been high in October as an uncertain economic environment dampens businesses' willingness to hire. A Labor Department spokesperson said, "A technical issue caused some data to be released prematurely," adding, "This issue is currently being corrected, and the complete data series will be released before the close of business on November 20, 2025." The data showed that the number of people continuing to claim unemployment benefits after the first week's claims (a proxy indicator for hiring) rose by 10,000 to 1.957 million in the week ending October 18, after seasonal adjustment. Continuing claims jumped significantly from 1.916 million in the week ending September 13. (Jinshi)

data.title: Nasdaq to Launch Global Listing Board by Mid-2026
data.description: According to Odaily, Nasdaq has announced plans to launch a global listing board by mid-2026. This initiative is part of a significant collaboration with the Singapore Exchange (SGX), aimed at simplifying the dual-listing process between the two markets.

data.title: Bullish reports Q3 earnings: Digital asset sales reach $41.6 billion
data.description: Digital asset trading platform Bullish announced its financial results for the third quarter ended September 30, 2025, with record highs in adjusted revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and adjusted net profit. Specifically, its digital asset sales reached $41.6 billion, compared to $54.2 billion in the same period of 2024. Net profit (loss) was $18.5 million, compared to a loss of $67.3 million in the same period of 2024. Adjusted revenue was $76.5 million, compared to $44.6 million in the same period of 2024. Adjusted trading revenue was $26.7 million, compared to $32.9 million in the same period of 2024. Adjusted net profit was $13.8 million, compared to a loss of $3.1 million in the same period of 2024. Adjusted EBITDA was $28.6 million, compared to $7.7 million in the same period of 2024. Bullish launched crypto options this quarter, and trading volume recently surpassed $1 billion. Furthermore, Bullish disclosed that its spot trading volume so far in the fourth quarter has increased by 77% compared to the third quarter. (Businesswire)

data.title: Hong Kong to Expand Tax Exemptions to Emerging Asset Classes
data.description: According to Odaily, Hong Kong's Secretary for Financial Services and the Treasury, Christopher Hui, revealed in a recent interview that the region is actively enhancing its tax exemption policies. The current exemptions, which apply to family offices and funds, are set to be extended to include emerging asset categories such as private credit, carbon credits, and digital assets. A bill is expected to be submitted to the Legislative Council next year to implement these changes.

data.title: Nasdaq: Global listing board to launch in mid-2026
data.description: Nasdaq announced that its global listing board will launch in mid-2026. It is understood that the Singapore Exchange (SGX) and Nasdaq have established a landmark partnership to launch the global listing board, simplifying dual-listing processes. (Jinshi)

data.title: U.S. Senate to Question CFTC Chair Nominee on Cryptocurrency Regulation
data.description:
According to PANews, U.S. senators are set to question Michael Selig on Wednesday regarding his reform plans on cryptocurrency regulation and election prediction markets. Selig, nominated by U.S. President Donald Trump as the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) chair, currently serves as the chief legal advisor for the SEC's cryptocurrency task force and advises Paul Atkins, the Republican SEC chairman appointed by Trump.

If Congress passes the CLARITY Act, the CFTC, responsible for regulating the commodity derivatives market, will play a more active role in the cryptocurrency industry. Selig is expected to be questioned about the CLARITY Act and how the CFTC plans to collaborate with the SEC, as well as regulate markets that allow Americans to bet on election outcomes and other events.

Traditionally, the CFTC is led by five bipartisan commissioners nominated by the president. Currently, the agency has only one commissioner, Republican Caroline Pham, who is serving as acting chair. Pham has stated that she will leave the agency once Trump's nominee for chair is confirmed.
